coredata.dc:title: Solvent-regulated synthesis of full-color fluorescent nitrogen/sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots for anti-counterfeiting textiles
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Yun
Bao, Xueming
Wu, Leilei
Zhou, Man
Yu, Yuanyuan
Wang, Qiang
Wang, Ping
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted widespread attention due to their unique optical, electronic,and luminescence properties. However, synthesisof highly-efficient panchromatic luminescent CQDs in aqueous solutions using non-benzenoid precursors remains a challenge. Herein, we proposed a simple one-step solvothermal strategy for developing full-color-emitting CQDs by the combined use of citric acid and thiourea. Through regulating the protonation extent of the solvent using water, glycerol and DMF, bright blue-, green-, yellow-, and red-emission CQDs were successfully synthetized, achieving the maximum emission wavelengths of 453, 531, 574 and 626 nm, respectively, and corresponds well to the calculated energy gap between HOMO and LUMO. Compared with those obtained from traditional methods, the fluorescence quantum yields of the prepared full-color CQDs were significantly increased by nitrogen-sulfur co-doping, reaching approximately 38.12 %, 32.50 %, 20.69 %, and 28.79 %, respectively. Moreover, introduction of reductive hydrazine hydrate stabilized the panchromaticluminescent characteristics of the full-color CQDs, avoiding excessive oxidation and even unacceptable blue-shift in the resulting emission spectra in aqueous solutions. Moreover, the synthesized full-color CQDs were successfully applied to fabricate anti-counterfeiting textiles by coating technique, achieving high durability and encouraging information encryption effect. It is noteworthy that the presented work constitutes an alternative for synthesis of full-color fluorescent CQDs and expands it potential applications for preparation of various anti-counterfeiting fiber-based products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Nitrogen-sulfur co-doping
Full-color fluorescent
Anti-counterfeiting textiles
HOMO-LUMO

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Solvothermal preparation of full-color emissive CQDs
Preparation of CQD-decorated cotton fabric with information encryption function
Determination ofMechanical Properties, washing resistance and pH values of CQD-decorated cotton fabrics
Morphology and structure characterization of the full-color CQDs
Stability and excitation mechanism of full-color CQDs under different conditions
Anti-counterfeiting effect of the full-color emissive CQD-decorated textiles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of solvent-regulated synthesis of full-color emissive CQDs through solvothermal method and preparation of the anti-counterfeiting textiles.

Morphology and structure of B-, G-, Y- and R-CQDs: (a-d) TEM, HRTEM images, (e-h) size distribution and (i) the corresponding absorption spectra, (j) XRD patterns, (k) Raman spectrum, and (l) FT-IR spectra.

(a) Full-scale XPS spectra of B-, G-, Y-, and R-CQDs, and (b-d) the corresponding high-resolution C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s XPS spectra. (e) C-O ratios and C = O ratios, (f) pyridinic N ratios and graphitic N ratios, and (g) –OH ratios and C = O ratios of the four CQDs.

3D-fluorescence spectra of B-, G-, Y- and R-CQDs: (a,b) before and after dialysis, (c) reduced by HH (the insets show the optical photographs under UV light). (d-g) Fluorescence intensity of B-, G-, Y-, and R-CQDs in HH solutions.

(a) Schematic representation of reduction process for the full-color CQDs, (b) fluorescence intensity of the HH-treated CQDs solutions (excited at 365 nm) during the continuous test, (c) zeta potential of the CQDs, (d) correlation between QY and oxygen-containing groups in CQD, (e) a comparison of the QYs of N-CQDs, S-CQDs or N,S-CQDs with previously reports [16,19,25,42,49,50].

(a) Illustration of the electronic transition and structure models of the full-color CQDs, (b, c) density functional theory calculation results: the calculated, and experimental obtained HOMO and LUMO, (d) 1H NMR spectra of the four types of CQDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of B-, G-, Y-, and R-CQD-decorated fabrics (excited at 365 nm); (b) the optimal excitation wavelength; (c) reflectance curve, (d) color depth, and (e) the chromaticity value of L, a* and b*; (f, g) fluorescence intensity of the fabric surface after storage at room temperature and under light irradiation, (h) bending resistance, and (i) CIE chromatograms of the CQD-coated textiles.

(a) Transparency of the paste and the fluorescent effect, with and without CQDs, (b) anti-counterfeiting illustrations of cotton fabrics, using B-CQD paste to encrypt rose patterns under sunlight, in the dark and under UV excitation, (c) images of blank slurry and CQD-printed cotton fabrics, under sunlight and UV excitation, (d) image of cotton fabric based on different CQDs for pattern matching, (e) information encryption on the B-CQD-decorated textiles by using programming binary.

head.ce:title: Solvent-regulated synthesis of full-color fluorescent nitrogen/sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots for anti-counterfeiting textiles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Science and Technology of Eco-Textile, Ministry of Education, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Science and Technology of Eco-Textile

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Science and Technology of Eco-Textile, Ministry of Education, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Science and Technology of Eco-Textile, the Ministry of Education, Jiangnan University, 1800 Lihu Avenue, Wuxi 214122, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Science and Technology of Eco-Textile

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted widespread attention due to their unique optical, electronic,and luminescence properties. However, synthesisof highly-efficient panchromatic luminescent CQDs in aqueous solutions using non-benzenoid precursors remains a challenge. Herein, we proposed a simple one-step solvothermal strategy for developing full-color-emitting CQDs by the combined use of citric acid and thiourea. Through regulating the protonation extent of the solvent using water, glycerol and DMF, bright blue-, green-, yellow-, and red-emission CQDs were successfully synthetized, achieving the maximum emission wavelengths of 453, 531, 574 and 626 nm, respectively, and corresponds well to the calculated energy gap between HOMO and LUMO. Compared with those obtained from traditional methods, the fluorescence quantum yields of the prepared full-color CQDs were significantly increased by nitrogen-sulfur co-doping, reaching approximately 38.12 %, 32.50 %, 20.69 %, and 28.79 %, respectively. Moreover, introduction of reductive hydrazine hydrate stabilized the panchromaticluminescent characteristics of the full-color CQDs, avoiding excessive oxidation and even unacceptable blue-shift in the resulting emission spectra in aqueous solutions. Moreover, the synthesized full-color CQDs were successfully applied to fabricate anti-counterfeiting textiles by coating technique, achieving high durability and encouraging information encryption effect. It is noteworthy that the presented work constitutes an alternative for synthesis of full-color fluorescent CQDs and expands it potential applications for preparation of various anti-counterfeiting fiber-based products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a type of carbon-based photoluminescence materials, which are spherical or spherical-like nanoparticles with an average size of 2–10 nm [1]. Due to the advantages of biocompatibility, chemical stability, excellent optical and electrical properties [2,3], CQDs have been widely used in detection [4], catalysis [5], biological imaging [6], and fluorescence labeling [7] and many other fields. The mostly used strategies for synthesizing CQDs include ‘top-down’ and ‘bottom-up’ methods [8], both of which can make CQDs have the graphene-like aggregated layers with diverse structures, oxygen-rich groups and sp2/sp3 carbon networks [9]. It has been reported that CQDs emitting from ultraviolet to near-infrared wavelengths can be well produced by adjusting the added precursors and the reaction conditions [10]. At present, most CQDs can be prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method, however, the products prepared by this method are not conducive to the emission of red fluorescence [11]. Meanwhile, the quantum yield (QY) of CQDs obtained by such method is not very high [12], and it is complicated to achieve separation and purification of the CQDs emitting specific fluorescence from the mixture system [13,14]. In recent years, CQDs with full-color luminescent have been synthesized by solvothermal method, but some products have potential toxicity due to the aromatic ring structure in the precursors [15,16]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a novel and feasible strategy to prepare eco-friendly CQDs with bright fluorescence and controllable emission wavelength.

Some researchers have demonstrated the efficient regulation effect of heteroatom doping on the photoluminescence ability of CQDs [17]. In addition to nitrogen doping, which has been extensively studied [18], sulfur doping can also modulatefluorescencecolor of the synthesized CQDs, thereby significantly increasing the content of graphite nitrogen and the corresponding QY [19,20]. Although there have been some reports on nitrogen- or sulfur-doped CQDs [21,22], there is relatively little work on achieving full-color luminescence of CQDs through nitrogen and sulfur co-doping. In addition, it is well known that the polarity and protonation level of the solvent used in the preparation also have a significant impact on the structure and fluorescence emission characteristics of CQDs [23]. Specifically, the protons generated in the high-temperature aqueous solution can inhibit the dehydration reaction, which easily leads to the destruction of graphite structure and the less possibility of red-light emission, thus most CQDs emit only blue light rather than colorful fluorescence [11]. In contrast, the CQDs with large conjugated domain size and narrow band gap can be easily prepared in low-polarity solvents with low protonation level [24]. For example, the non-proton solvent of DMF is more conducive to the dehydration and carbonization of CQD precursors than the protonic solvents such as water, thus increasing the conjugated p-domain size and redshift emission band of CQDs [25]. Moreover, formamide not only has sufficient active site and low steric hindrance, but also has a protective effect on graphite structure, contributing to the formation of sp2 conjugate domains [26]. Therefore, it is possible to achieve the preparation of CQDs with adjustable fluorescence color and high photochemical stability [27,28], which will have great development potential in the field of textiles with anti-counterfeiting and information encryption functions.

In recent years, with the development of science and technology, textiles have broken through the basic functions of warmth, comfort and beauty, gradually moving towards functionality and intelligence, such as fuel purification [29], ultraviolet shielding [30], and luminescence [31]. Fluorescent materials such as CQDs are relatively popular in the preparation of anti-counterfeiting textiles. In this work, a simple one-step solvothermal strategy for developing full-color-emitting CQDs was proposed, using citric acid and thiourea as the carbon and nitrogen-sulfur doping sources, respectively. By selectively using solvents with different polarity and protonation levels, full-chromatographic luminescent CQDs were prepared, followed by the construction of anti-counterfeiting textiles. Briefly, as shown in Fig. 1 , blue-, green-, red-, and yellow-emissive CQDs (named B-, G-, R-, Y-CQDs, respectively) were synthesized in water, glycerol, DMF, and their mixed solutions, respectively. Based on the optimal dosage ratio and reaction conditions (Fig. S1), well-dispersed full-color CQDs in aqueous solution were obtained, and the corresponding fluorescence emission wavelengthwas stabilized by adding hydrazine hydrate. What’s more, the luminescence mechanism of full-color CQDs was well verified by the calculatedHOMO-LUMOenergy gap. In addition, an anti-counterfeiting textile with information encryption function was successfully prepared by coating method. The presented work provides an efficient strategy for the synthesis of full-color emissive CQDs, and expands their application in the structural design and construction of anti-counterfeiting textiles.

Citric acid (CA) and thiourea (TU) were supplied by Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co. (China). N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), glycerol, and 85 % hydrazine hydrate (HH) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (China). Scoured cotton fabric (378 g/m2) was supplied by Shanghai Textile Industry Institute of Technical Supervision (China), and a commercial thickeners of DM-5262HB and an adhesive of DM-5128 were commercially obtained from Dymatic Co. (China). Other reagents were of analytical grade and commercially available.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solvothermal preparation of full-color emissive CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Full-color emissive CQDs were synthesized by solvothermal method using citric acid and thiourea. Briefly, blue light-emissive CQDs (B-CQDs) were prepared according to the following procedure. Firstly, 8 mmol citric acid and 40 mmol thiourea was added into 20 mL of distilled water. Subsequently, the mixture solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 6 h to realize carbonization and surface passivation. Based on the same conditions, green-, red-, and yellow-emissive CQDs were synthesized by using glycerol, DMF, and their mixture (7:3) as solvents, respectively. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained CQD dispersions were purified by dialysis using a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off: 1000 Da) for 72 h to remove residual precursors, then freeze-dried. In order to eliminate the adverse effects of CQD oxidation during dialysis, 50 µL/mL of HH was added into 0.4 mg/mL of different CQD solutions, respectively. Eventually, stable B-CQD, G-CQD, Y-CQDs and R-CQD solutions were obtained after 3 days of reduction at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQD-decorated cotton fabric with information encryption function
ce:section.ce:para: Stable B-CQD, G-CQD, Y-CQDs and R-CQD solutions were used to prepare modified textiles through two methods including dipping and printing. For the dipping method, the fabric was incubated with the prepared CQD solutions at room temperature for 30 min, then dried at 80 °C. On the other hand, 1.1 g thickener and 6.25 g adhesive were added to 10 mL of the four CQD solutions, respectively, then mixed and stirred at a speed of 400 r/min to obtain printing pastes with different fluorescence, ranging from blue to red emission. Finally, the pastes were evenly printed on the cotton fabrics through screen printing method, and the textiles with information encryption function were obtained after drying at 80 °C.

The absorption of the synthesized CQDs was examined using a UV-1800 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, China), the fluorescence spectra of CQDs and CQD-modified fabrics were recorded using an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Morphology and sizes of different CQDs were examined using a JEM-2100plus transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan) with a 200 kV operating voltage. Zeta potential for each CQD sample was measured by a NanoBrook Omni particle-size analyzer (Brookhaven, US). The chemical structures of different CQDs were characterized by K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo, US), D2 PHASER X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Bruker, Germany), Nexus 470 Fourier Transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Nicolet, USA), and inVia-Reflex Raman spectroscopy (Renishaw, UK). Moreover, an AVANCE III 500 NMR measurement (Bruker, Germany) was used to investigate the characteristic structures of CQD samples. Color parameters of the cotton fabrics with different emission spectrum of CQDs were determined using a DC850 spectrophotometer (Datacolor, USA), and the color depth and the chromatic coordinate values of L, a*, and b* were recorded, respectively.

Quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 was used as a reference substance [32,33], the fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of the prepared CQDs were determined and calculated by a relative method as Eq. (1).
(1)
QY = Q
Y 1
×
F
F 1
×
A 1
A
×
η
η 1
2
where QY and QY1 represent the quantum yield of the testing sample and the referenced fluorescence dyes (quinine sulfate), respectively. F is the integrated emission intensity of the test sample, η is the refractive index of the solvent (water is 1.33), and A is the absorbance at the excitation wavenumber (360 nm) ranging from 0 to 0.1. The subscript ‘1′ refers to the reference parameter value of quinine sulfate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination ofMechanical Properties, washing resistance and pH values of CQD-decorated cotton fabrics
ce:section.ce:para: Tensile strength and fracture elongation of the CQD-modified textiles were measured according to ASTM D5035-2006. In addition, washing resistance of the different textiles was determined after 5 times washing, each washing was carried out with 2.0 g/L detergent at 40 °C for 9 min. To determine the pH value of each textile, 2 g of fabric fragments were incubated with 100 mL of distilled water in a beaker, and the pH value of the extracted solution was examined according to GB/T 7573–2009.

Full geometry optimizations and frequency analysis for all models considered were performed using density functional theory (DFT). All theoretical calculations were employed using Gaussian 09 program, and the bandgap energies of CQDs were calculated according to the Tauc plots as Eq. (2).
(2)
h ν α
1 / n
= A
( h ν - E g )
where α and hν are the absorption coefficient and the energy of the incident light, respectively, and n is 2 for the CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure characterization of the full-color CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM images in Fig. 2 a–d shows the microscopy of the four different CQDs, and all HRTEM images reveal that B-, G-, Y-, and R-CQDs were uniformly dispersed and homogeneous, indicating high crystallinity of the CQDs. The four types of CQDs show a well-resolved lattice spacing of approximately 0.21 nm, which corresponds well to the (100) crystal plane in the graphite structure. Specifically, the lattice stripe distances of Y- and R-CQDs are about 0.24 nm, corresponding to the (002) crystal surface in the graphite structure. The results in Fig. 2e–h indicate that the average diameters of the four CQDs reached approximately 2.09, 2.53, 3.08, and 4.10 nm, respectively, revealing that the redshift of fluorescence emission band from sample B-CQDs to R-CQDs corresponds to the gradual increase in the particle size.

Fig. 2i displays the UV–vis absorption of CQDs ranging from 300 to 700 nm, which is directly related to the photoluminescence emission. Meanwhile, the insets show the physical images of the four CQDs under visible light and ultraviolet excitation. For the B-, G-, Y- and R-CQDs, the absorption peak at approximately 350 mm is usually owing to the n-π* electron transition of the surface state from C = O, C-OH, and C = N [8,19]. The peak appearing at around 465 nm is mainly attributed to the n-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 system from C = O group [34,35]. In addition, an increasingly significant broad absorption was observed at approximately 560 nm from B- to R-CQDs, which is due to the n-π* transition of surface states containing C = N, C = O, C-O, and C-S structures, as well as the conjugated structure adsorbed on the C = N band [36]. Therefore, it is inferred that red emission is closely related to the functional groups in the CQDs.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns in Fig. 2j shows that the four CQDs contain amorphous structure with an obvious broad peak appearing at approximately 23.33°, which belongs to (100) reflection. Also, in the spectra of G-, Y- and R-CQDs, two new characteristic peaks corresponding to the (002) facet appear at 19.39° and 27.19°, implying that the highly conjugated sp2 domain was tightly packed within the carbon core of the CQDs [37,38], which is consistent well with the result shown in HRTEM images. The lowest diffraction peak intensity of B-CQDs was observed, indicating that the effective conjugation length of B-CQD is shorter than that of the other three. As for Y- and R-CQDs, the related diffraction peaks became rather narrower and sharper, revealing a significant increase in polycrystalline structure [26], which was never reported before. Moreover, few nonfluorescent carbide precipitates with the characteristic peaks were observed at approximately 14.76°, 24.58°, 27.19° and 38.22°, indicating that graphitization was accompanied by functionalization, and the precursors were successively condensed, dehydrated and carbonized in the solvent. That is, the precursors formed tiny spherical nuclei through aggregation after dehydration, and the remaining precursors further aggregated on the surface of the initially formed nucleus, ultimately forming CQDs [39].

The Raman spectra of G-, Y-, and R-CQDs show two peaks at approximately 1360 and 1640 cm−1 (Fig. 2k), which correspond to the disordered (D band) and graphitic carbon domains (G band), respectively, indicating the coexistence of sp3- and sp2-hybridized carbon in the three types of CQDs. ID/IG ratio is an evaluation indicator of the degree of graphitization, where ID
, IG represent the Raman intensity related to the defect sites and graphitic sites, respectively [40]. The obtained results reveal that the graphitization of G-, Y- and R-CQDs were noticeably increased compared to that of B-CQDs. Specially, the ID/IG ratio of R-CQDs (ID/IG = 0.8047) is significantly higher than that of the other CQDs, indicating that R-CQDs has a high graphite-like structure of backbone and carbon core, which is highly consistent with XRD results. The chemicalstructureandcomposition of CQDs were further demonstrated by FT-IR spectroscopy. As can been seen in Fig. 2l, two peaks attributed to the stretching vibration of –OH and –NH2 bands were observed in the range of 3500–3000 cm−1. The weak peak appearing at 2883 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of sp2 C-H, and the stretching vibration of carbonyl group (−C = O) at 1713 cm−1 verified the newly formation of –COOH and C = O-NH2. In addition, the peaks of Y- and R-CQDs at 1643 cm−1 were more significant when compared to that of B-CQDs, suggesting a positive correlation between redshift emission and C = N stretching. Meanwhile, due to the involvement of the sulfur element, the peaks attributed to the tensile vibration of C = S, C-S and C-S-O stretches were detected at 1189 cm−1, 840 cm−1 and 635 cm−1, respectively. According to the characteristic peaks, it can be drawn that the prepared CQDs are composed of aromatic sp2 conjugated domain, and N and O related surface groups.

Elemental compositions and surface functional groups of the CQDs were further analyzed using XPS. As depicted in Fig. 3 a, the co-doped N and S elements were observed in all CQDs. The high-resolution C 1 s spectra in Fig. 3b reveals the presence of C = C/C-C (284.2 eV), C-O/C-N (285.8 eV), and C = O/C = N (288.0 eV). Meanwhile, all N 1 s spectra in Fig. 3c can be resolved into three individual characteristic peaks, corresponding to pyridinic N (398.4 eV), pyrrolic N (399.4 eV) and graphitic N (400.3 eV), respectively. In the high-resolution O 1 s spectra (Fig. 3d), three peaks were detected at 530.4, 531.6 eV and 532.9 eV, which correspond to –OH, C-O and C = O groups, respectively. The obtained results indicate that the obtained CQDs were a mixture of similar structures that integrated different sizes of conjugated sp2 carbon domains (fused rings) and sulfur-doped thiophene units (Fig. S2) [41].

The Fig. 3e–g depict the bonding ratios of different polar groups in the prepared CQDs. For the R-CQDs, the C = O bonding ratio (38 %) in carbon-containing groups and graphitic N ratio (56 %) were significantly higher than those of the Y-, G-, and B-CQDs. Due to the doping of N atoms, the fluorescence emission wavelength of CQDs underwent a redshift, resulting in the emission of four different fluorescence. This is mainly due to the electron doping effect, which reduces the energy level gap between electrons [42]. It can be seen that the pyridinic N ratio decreased with the increase of C = N ratio, indicating that the nitrogen-containing groups are important functional groups for inducing luminescence. Moreover, the proportion of graphite N gradually increased from B- to R-CQDs and significantly increased in R-CQDs, suggesting that graphite N is an important factor inducing the fluorescence redshift of CQDs. Overall, graphitic N is an electron-donating element that can produce an intermediate band gap state in the original band gap, which can effectively narrow the band gap of CQDs, resulting in an increase in fluorescence redshift and graphitization. The N contents in the four CQDs increased from 2.02 % to 16.87 %, while the C content decreased from 68.11 % to 58.12 % (Fig. 3a), further confirming the above inference. Besides, it is worth noting that the R-CQDs had a higher oxygen content and oxidation degree than the other three, which allow the surface carboxyl groups to provide more opportunities for local distortion of sp2 hybrid carbon atoms, thereby narrowing the energy gap and fluorescence redshift.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and excitation mechanism of full-color CQDs under different conditions
ce:section.ce:para: In order to better explore the fluorescence properties of four types of CQDs, we characterized their optical properties through three-dimensional (3D) excitation-emission maps, and the results are depicted in Fig. 4 a–c. As shown in Fig. 4d, the emission peak of B-CQDs is mainly distributedfrom 440 nm to 460 nm with the excitation wavelength ranging from 350 nm to 400 nm. For the G-CQDs, the emission peaks slightly changed from 507 nm to 552 nm as the excitation wavelength shifted from 390 nm to 420 nm (Fig. 4e). In the spectra of Y-CQDs, two peaks appearing at 526 nm and 586 nm were observed (Fig. 4f). When the excitation wavelength shifted from 440 nm to 510 nm, the peak intensity at 526 nm decreased, while the peak intensity at 586 nm increased simultaneously. In the case of R-CQDs (Fig. 4g), the maximum peak was located at approximately 626 nm when the excitation wavelength shifted from 490 nm to 570 nm. The results demonstrate that G-CQDs have a certain excitation dependence, while the other three CQDs exhibit excitation-independent emissioncharacteristics. This may be because a slight change in the level of solvent protonation can induce a red shift in fluorescence emission, and produce varying degrees of such favorable effects. Notably, the fluorescence emission spectra of the four CQDs after dialysis are all in the blue light region, which is due to the oxidation of the synthesized CQDs in aqueous solution. The structure of unstable functional groups on the surface of CQD was changed by oxidation, and the C = O group was converted into carboxyl group, companying by opening the furan ring to form more carboxyl groups. As a result, small aromatic clusters connecting carboxyl terminal residues were formed, and the carbon nucleus structures were damaged, leading to higher disorder [3]. Accordingly, such self-oxidation evidently weakens the red emission center, and strengthens the blue-emission center of the full-color CQDs.

In order to obtain stable full-color fluorescent CQDs, a reducing agent of HH was added into the dispersion solution after dialysis, and the reduction process is presented in Fig. 5 a. The results in Fig. S3-S6 indicate that the CQDs oxidized during dialysis were successfully reduced and exhibited excellent storage stability, maintaining their initial fluorescence emission states. This can be interpreted as that during the HH treatment, most C = O groups in CQDs were reduced to C-O and the density of sp2 C increased simultaneously, producing a broader co-aromatic carbon network and a smaller density of oxygen groups [43], which can be verified by the declining C/O ratio demonstrated in Fig. S7. Besides, both acidic and alkaline conditions can cause redshift in the emission wavelength of G-, Y- and R-CQDs, especially under strongly alkaline conditions (Fig. S8), reaching 14 nm, 45 nm and 123 nm, respectively. After incubation with HH at pH 13, the emission wavelength of G-CQDs reached 546 nm, which was redshifted by 15 nm compared to that without adding HH. The noticeable redshift is mainly attributed to conversion of C = N to C-N in CQDs, resulting in a change in luminescent color. Thus, HH plays a dual role during the reduction process, i.e., it provides an alkaline environment for the redshift of CQDs, and reduces the oxidized CQDs to their initial luminous colors as well.

For further exploring the stability of the CQDs after reduction, the sustained excitation stability of fluorescence intensity and zeta potential for each sample were also examined. Specifically, the excitation stabilities of the CQDs were concerned by calculating the percentage of fluorescence intensity decay after 2 h of sustained excitation at 365 nm. After the reduction treatment, the initial luminous colors of the CQDs could be well-recovered, and the corresponding fluorescence intensities remained at 99.53 %, 94.32 %, 98.66 %, and 94.03 % (Fig. 5b), respectively, which are close to the original values before dialysis (Fig. S9). Moreover, the results in Fig. 5c indicate that the electronegativity of the synthesized CQDs decreased sequentially from B- to R-CQDs. After reduction, the zeta potentials of CQDs were slightly decreased to −35.3, −34.9, −31.3, and −29.5 mV, respectively, mainly attributed to the deprotonation of –COOH distributed on the CQD surfaces [44,45]. On the whole, the obtained results ensure the stability and reliability of the prepared CQDs in the processing of functional textiles.

The co-doping of sulfur and nitrogen in carbon framework has a significant impact on the CQDs properties. The QY of different CQDs was also examined and the result are depicted in Fig. 5d. It can be seen that QY is closely related to the content of oxygen-containing groups in the prepared CQDs. Generally, a high percentage of C-N configurations in B-CQDs as electron-donating species might enhance the QY by inhibiting nonradiative transitions. For the B-, G-, Y- and R-CQDs, the obtained QYs reached 38.12 %, 32.50 %, 20.69 %, and 28.79 %, respectively, which are noticeably higher than the yield reported in most previous reports (Fig. 5e) [19,46]. Comparatively, the QY of R-CQDs appears to be a bit low, possibly due to the presence of large amounts of electron-withdrawing carbonyl functional groups acting as non-radiative trap centers [47].

In order to validate the critical role of functional groups of CQDs in regulating its absorption and emission spectra, DFT calculations related to the HOMO-LUMO energy level of four CQDs were proceeded, and the electronic transition processes of model CQDs are shown in Fig. 6 a. In combination with the structural analysis in XPS, the increasing content of the C = N leads to more electron transitions and redshift emission. In detail, as an electron-donating group, –OH can slightly improve the HOMO-LUMO gap, while C = N and C = O as electron-withdrawing groups can significantly reduce the gap [15]. Therefore, the C = N group can introduce more new energy levels to the electronic structure, thereby leading to a red shift in the emission of CQDs. Another phenomenon can be observed in Fig. 6b, where the band gap energies of B-, G-, Y-, and R-CQDs decreased from 3.22 eV to 2.06 eV, which probably due to the increased degree of graphitization and the size of the sp2-domain [48]. This is consistent with the theory that the longer the wavelength, the lower the energy [18,26]. It should be noted that the theoretical calculated values of Eg were a bit higher than those from the experimental calculation, (i.e., the values obtained from tangent lines in the Tauc plot), which was mainly due to the fact that the origin mode chosen for calculation was the fragment structure in CQDs (Fig. 6c). Meanwhile, the energy gap of CQDs was negatively correlated with the corresponding particle size, that is, the energy gap decreases with the increase of the particle size of CQDs. Indeed, by increasing the effects of both the p-p conjugate domains and the surface state, the HOMO-LUMO energy gap will be further reduced which is beneficial for the emission of red fluorescence.

The chemical structure of the full-color emissive CQDs were characterized by 1H NMR, and the obtained spectra are shown in Fig. 6d. The signal at 0.8–1.37 ppm is attributed to the hydrogen in –CH3 and –CH2, and signals at 3–5 and 2–3 ppm are assigned to hydroxyl (–OH)/amino (–NH2) and carbonyl/C-S groups, respectively. Furthermore, the signal from allylbenzene was detected at approximately 6.7 ppm, which confirms the increased degree of graphitization and tighter structure upon increasing the nitrogen content from B- to R-CQDs. Moreover, from B- to R-CQDs, a small peak at 8.5 ppm appeared and gradually moved to a low field at 9.0 ppm, indicating the presence of hydrogen bonding [7]. Overall, the results from 1H NMR further confirm that the increase in the contents of C = O and nitrogen element leads to the redshift of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-counterfeiting effect of the full-color emissive CQD-decorated textiles
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the potential application of the full-color CQDs, fluorescent anti-counterfeiting textiles were prepared by screen printing method using the CQD-containing pastes. As shown in Fig. S10, the obtained fabrics exhibit significant blue, green, yellow, and red fluorescence under UV excitation. The fluorescence characteristics of the fabric samples were characterized, and the result are depicted in Fig. 7 a. An obvious peak was observed in the blue light-emitting area, and the peak intensities from B-CQDs to R-CQDs gradually increased in the range of 560–660 nm, supporting the multi-color fluorescence effect of the textiles. Meanwhile, at their respective optimal excitation wavelengths (Fig. 7b), the maximum emission wavelengths of the four CQDs were similar to the results in Fig. 4d-g. A blue shift deviation of about 25 nm between the maximum emission wavelength of each CQD-modified fabric and the corresponding CQD solution was detected, mainly attributed to a slight influence of fabric itself on the wavelength generated by the excitation. Furthermore, in the visible wavelength range, there were no significant differences in the brightness (L), red-green (a*), and yellow-blue (b*) between the fabric samples, and the color depths (K/S) and reflectance vales were at similar levels (Fig. 7c-e). This effectively demonstrates that the CQDs hardly interfered with the original color appearances of the textiles, and has great potential in the field of anti-counterfeiting.

The structureandmorphology of the modified fabrics treated by dipping method were also examined by means of FT-IR, XRD and SEM. As can be seen in Fig. S11, the difference of FTIR spectra of the modified fabrics was not significant, mainly due to the fact that the functional group of the four CQDs was basically the same, which is consistence well with the results shown in Fig. 2i. In addition, compared with the untreated, a new broad peak at 2θ = 23.33° appeared in the XRD spectra of the CQD-modified fabrics (Fig. S11b) [51], confirming the effective deposition of the CQDs on fiber surfaces and matching well with the results shown in Fig. 2j. SEM results showed that some uniformly dispersed deposits were observed on the fiber surfaces of the CQD-modified textiles (Fig. S11c), which provides good evidence for the uniform fluorescence emission characteristics of anti-counterfeiting textiles.

Besides, the storage stability of the CQD-decorated textiles was also examined. As shown in Fig. 7f, after being stored at room temperature for 1 month, the fluorescence properties of the fabrics remained almost unchanged. To investigate the stability of fluorescence emission intensity, each CQD-decorated fabric was irradiated with a xenon lamp under different light intensity. After 30 min of irradiation with the light intensity of 1 sun (approximately 100 mW/cm2), the change in the fluorescence intensity of the fabric samples was not remarkable (Fig. 7g). With the increase of light intensity, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs on the fabrics decreased slightly. The data in Fig. 7h reveals that the bending resistances of all CQD-decorated textiles are at a similar level as that of the untreated, exhibiting an acceptable soft feeling. More visual chrominance data of the prepared CQDs are depicted in the CIE chromaticity diagram (Fig. 7i) and Table S1.

With the increasing attention on information security, the smart textiles with fluorescence anti-counterfeiting and information encryption functions have broad application prospects [52,53]. By the combined use of the four CQDs as fluorescent materials, their potential applications in graphic security and information encryption are investigated. As shown in Fig. 8 a, for the CQD-containing printing paste, we placed it on a glass slide and flattened it with a cover glass, and the ‘lamb’ pattern below could be clearly observed, demonstrating excellent transparency and significant fluorescence effect. In terms of pattern anti-counterfeiting, it can be seen in Fig. 8b, the pattern on the cotton fabric were invisible in the dark, while the glowing rose can be clearly observed under UV light. Furthermore, after the B-, G-, Y-, and R-CQD-containing pastes were printed on the cotton fabrics in the form of letters ‘JNU’ (Fig. 8c), the four colorful CQDs might be well presented on the fabric surfaces under UV excitation, while the color appearance of each fabric was relatively low in sunlight. In addition, a variety of complex and even multi-color patterns can also be encrypted by blending the prepared CQD pastes (Fig. 8d). Hereon, B- and R-CQDs were mixed together to obtain a purple luminescent paste, and B-, G-, and R-CQDs were combined to form a ‘colorful rose’ that presented a bright color under UV excitation. In addition, the CQD-based luminous cotton fabrics can also be used for information encryption, which we demonstrated by programming binaries. As shown in Fig. 8e, the CQD-containing paste was represented by ‘1′ and the paste without fluorescence was represented by ‘0′. Firstly, we printed an incorrect password with the pink pigment (as shown above), then used the CQD paste to print the correct password on the fabric surface. According to binary rules, the incorrect password under sunlight is ‘G92m’. Only under UV excitation can the correct ‘JNU’ information be decrypted well. Thus, the emitted fluorescence can effectivelymask the original-colored patterns, suggesting that such full-color emissive CQDs with information encryption properties have potential applications in the fields of diverse anti-counterfeiting textiles.

Mechanical properties of the anti-counterfeiting textiles were also explored. As shown in Fig. S12, the tensile strengths of the fabric samples were similar to that of the blank sample, accompanying with a slight decrease in the air permeability (Fig. S13). Besides, durability of the modified textiles was also examined, and there wasonlyaslightdecreasein the fluorescence intensity for each modified fabric after 5 time washing, due to the presence of printing layer (Fig. S14). The pH values of the modified fabrics after extraction in aqueous solution were at approximately 7.5, which meet the requirements of eco-textiles according to Oeko-Tex Standard 100.

Through regulating the protonation extent of the solvents using water, glycerol and DMF, we successfully developed a simple one-step solvothermal strategy for synthesizing full-color-emitting CQDs. Fluorescence quantum yield of the prepared full-color CQDs was significantly enhanced by nitrogen-sulfur co-doping, reaching up to approximately 38.12 %, while using citric acid and thiourea as the carbon and nitrogen-sulfur doping sources, respectively. Stable panchromatic luminescence and high stability of blue, green, yellow, and red light-emissive CQDs were achieved by adding a highly reducing substance of hydrazine hydrate, avoiding the excessive oxidation during storage. The luminescence mechanism of the full-color CQDs was evidently verified through the HOMO-LUMO theoretical model, where the energy gap from B- to R-CQDs gradually decreased from 3.22 eV to 2.06 eV. In addition, a panchromatic information anti-counterfeiting method is proposed to achieve stable luminescence on textiles by reducing self-oxidized CQDs, which was supported by the practical applications in this work. The anti-counterfeiting color matching and high-quality information encryption have been realized on the cotton textiles by using CQD-containing pastes. Overall, the presented technique provides an alternative for achieving advanced security encryption and developing the CQD-based smart textiles.

Yun Yuan: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Xueming Bao: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Leilei Wu: Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Man Zhou: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Yuanyuan Yu: Supervision, Project administration. Qiang Wang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Ping Wang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the Six Talent Peaks Projects in Jiangsu Province (no. XCL-133), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22371094), and Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX24_0000).

ce:section.ce:para: Detailed information on the emission, 3D-fluorescence and XPS survey spectras of prepared CQDs, Color appearance and air and moisture permeabilities of the four CQD-coated cotton textiles.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.152488.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
